objective: Diagnose sibling-reported 504 on POST /api/lambda/functions/pth-sam2 (+ post-restore 502 flaps) and drive smoke_cvat_sam2.py to an honest PASS or a sealed FAIL with root cause, non-destructively.

primary.symptom: 504 Gateway Timeout on POST /api/lambda/functions/pth-sam2; post-restore 502 flaps
primary.cause: Docker Linux engine was DOWN at the time of the sibling probe. The 'docker-desktop' WSL2 distro was in state=Stopped while the Docker Desktop.exe host process still ran, so no engine pipe (npipe:////./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine) existed. With the engine down, traefik/CVAT/nuclio were unreachable or flapping, producing 504/502 at the gateway. This is consistent with prior daemon crashes on the constrained WSL2 backend (see Plan/DOCKER_RUNTIME_AND_SESSION_USE.md 6b build-safety note).
primary.evidence: docker info failed with 'open //./pipe/dockerDesktopLinuxEngine: The system cannot find the file specified'; wsl -l -v showed docker-desktop=Stopped; no *docker* named pipes present.
primary.fix: Non-destructive full restart of Docker Desktop (Docker Desktop.exe -Shutdown, then engine re-init). Engine came back at server 29.6.1; docker-desktop distro=Running; all 39 containers (production CVAT 2.24 + nuclio + pth-sam2 + cvat269) auto-started. No volumes wiped, no prune.

secondary.symptom: Intermittent fast 503 Service Unavailable on POST /api/lambda/functions/pth-sam2 after the engine was healthy (my back-to-back re-runs).
secondary.cause: pth-sam2 is the CPU SAM2 interactor (single worker: numWorkers=1/maxWorkers=1; HostConfig NanoCpus=4e9 -> 4 CPUs, Memory=8GiB). Docker healthcheck is aggressive: interval=1s, timeout=3s, default 3 retries against nuclio processor readiness endpoint http://127.0.0.1:8082/ready. During a ~20-37s CPU inference, torch saturates all 4 cores and starves the nuclio Go health server, so /ready misses/refuses several consecutive probes -> Docker marks the container 'unhealthy' -> CVAT returns 503 to any invoke landing in that recovery window. Once inference finishes and CPU frees, /ready recovers, health returns to 'healthy', and the next invoke succeeds. Not OOM (OOMKilled=false) and not a crash (RestartCount=0).
secondary.evidence: Health.Log showed alternating exit 0 and 'dial tcp 127.0.0.1:8082: connect: connection refused' entries; nuclio dashboard reported state=ready throughout; RestartCount=0; OOMKilled=false.
secondary.mitigation: Non-destructive: gate invocations on Docker health=healthy and space them out. A single invoke from a healthy state succeeds. Client timeout is already generous (tool uses requests timeout=120s); the failures are fast gateway 503s, not client timeouts, so raising the client timeout does not help. No config edits to upstream nuclio/CVAT were made (health-probe tuning would require an upstream override and is out of non-destructive scope).

actions_taken: Live-probed docker info / context / processes / named pipes; found engine down (docker-desktop distro Stopped).
Non-destructively restarted Docker Desktop; polled engine to readiness (server 29.6.1).
Verified CVAT about=2.24.0 and nuclio pth-sam2 state=ready; 39 containers up.
Ran tools/smoke_cvat_sam2.py; first run PASS (exit 0, latency 23.304s).
Diagnosed intermittent 503 via docker inspect health log + healthcheck config (1s/3s probe vs CPU-saturated single worker).
Ran a health-gated retry loop; obtained a second honest PASS (exit 0, latency 32.401s).
Did NOT wipe volumes, prune, or point production bridge at cvat269.

smoke_runs.note: back-to-back re-run during post-inference health flap

final_smoke_report_on_disk.note: On-disk report is an honest PASS with all checks true. Per task warning it may be a stale/concurrent write from a sibling; this seal instead relies on this agent's own verified exit codes and latencies above. Deterministic mask (foreground_pixels=21491) is consistent across all passing runs and the on-disk report.

honesty_notes: pth-sam2 here is the CPU interactor, not GPU. The interactor works end-to-end (CVAT -> nuclio -> SAM2 -> binary 256x256 mask). Residual intermittent 503s under rapid re-invocation are a Docker healthcheck sensitivity to CPU saturation, not a functional failure; a single invoke from a healthy state is reliable.
